behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely in the filtering predicate — does the filter function drop out-of-stock items before or after scoring? This is a pure function of stock status; no module boundary is crossed. REAL-INFRA: An in-process list of product stubs with stock flags is sufficient to reproduce the failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the product list is safe because the assertion is about the filtering rule, not the data source. LLM-DEP: No ML involved. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the bug appears in a single filter predicate, not at a module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Create a product list containing items with varying relevance scores where some are marked out-of-stock. Pass this list through the stock-availability filter. Assert that the returned collection contains no out-of-stock products and that every in-stock product with a valid score is present.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the category-exclusion predicate — does the filter correctly compare product category against the blocked-categories set from user settings? This is a pure function of (product, user_preferences). REAL-INFRA: No I/O required; user preferences and product categories can be provided as in-process stubs. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the preference object is safe because correctness depends on the comparison logic, not on how preferences are loaded. LLM-DEP: No ML. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — user preferences are passed as input, so no module handoff is needed to reproduce the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a user preference object with one or more explicitly blocked categories and a product list that spans blocked and allowed categories. Invoke the preference filter. Assert that no product from a blocked category appears in the output and that all allowed-category products are retained.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The specific failure is in the scoring component that weights browsing signals — does a product with a browsing-match score higher than one without? If the scoring function accepts (product, user_browsing_history) as inputs, the failure is entirely inside that function. REAL-INFRA: No external I/O; browsing history can be injected. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the browsing data is appropriate since the claim is about the weighting rule, not the data pipeline. LLM-DEP: The description implies a rule-based or collaborative-filtering algorithm, not a generative model. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic was considered because of 'similar-user behavior', but the behavior under test is specifically 'browsing patterns vs. no signal' — a deterministic weighting that does not depend on non-deterministic model output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fixed set of products: some whose categories and attributes match a synthetic user browsing history, others with no match. Feed both the product set and browsing history into the ranking function. Assert that every browsing-matched product ranks strictly above every no-signal product in the output ordering.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the pagination function's sort key and slice logic — if the sort is non-deterministic (e.g., relies on hash-map iteration order or unstable sort), the same page 2 call returns different results. This is entirely within the pagination/ordering routine. REAL-INFRA: An in-process fixed dataset is sufficient; no database is required to reproduce ordering instability in the algorithm. MOCK-HIDE: No hidden dependency — the dataset is the full input. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered because ordering might vary across a real DB cursor, but the behavior explicitly conditions on 'underlying data hasn't changed' — algorithmic determinism is the failure, not storage behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Produce a fixed in-memory ranked product list. Request page 2 twice via the pagination function with identical parameters and an unchanged dataset. Assert that both calls return the same product IDs in the same order. Also verify that page 2 does not overlap with page 1.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the serendipity injection step — does the algorithm include at least one product from outside the user's browsed categories in its final output? This is a post-processing property of the ranked list. REAL-INFRA: No I/O needed; user browsing history and product catalog are pure inputs. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking is safe because correctness is about the output composition, not the data source. LLM-DEP: The serendipity requirement reads as a designed business rule (a 'must inject novelty' step), not an emergent ML property. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected because the test asserts a structural property of the output (presence of an unseen category), which a deterministic rule must guarantee regardless of model behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a user browsing history covering a known set of categories, and a product catalog that includes items from both browsed and unvisited categories. Run the full recommendation function. Assert that the result set contains at least one product whose category does not appear in the user's browsing history.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the cold-start branch — when the algorithm detects an empty history it should fall through to a popularity ranking rather than returning nothing. This is a conditional path in the recommendation function. REAL-INFRA: An in-memory popularity index stub is sufficient; no real storage needed. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the popularity data is appropriate because the test is about the fallback path, not the popularity computation. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered because popularity data might live in a separate module, but the cold-start routing logic itself is what fails — feeding in a stub popularity list to the algorithm's cold-start path is enough to reproduce the bug.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the recommendation engine with a new user profile that has empty purchase history and empty browsing history, along with a stub popularity-ranked product list. Invoke the recommendation function. Assert that the result is non-empty, and that products are drawn from the popularity ranking (e.g., top-N popular products appear in the output).

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the boundary between the click-event handler and the persistence layer — does the handler correctly extract product ID, list position, and timestamp from the click event and write all three fields to storage? A unit test of the handler in isolation cannot confirm the fields are persisted correctly. REAL-INFRA: An in-process fake store (in-memory repository) is sufficient; no real database required to reproduce incorrect field mapping. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the persistence layer entirely would let a wrongly-constructed write call pass — the integration with a real (or realistic fake) store is needed. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected — a real database provides no additional fidelity over an in-process fake for verifying field correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the click-tracking handler to an in-memory event store. Simulate a click event carrying a product ID, a list position, and a known timestamp. Invoke the handler. Query the store and assert that exactly one record was written containing the correct product ID, the correct position index, and a timestamp that matches the event.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Performance under realistic load cannot be caught by unit or integration tests because in-process stubs eliminate I/O latency, cache-miss costs, query-planner overhead, and serialization time — all of which dominate response time in production. REAL-INFRA: The failure only manifests with a real catalog store (database or search index) under real query conditions; an in-memory Python list of 100k dicts does not reproduce the actual bottleneck. MOCK-HIDE: An in-memory fake catalog will almost always pass a 200ms budget even if the real query path is 1-2 seconds. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because the performance failure is caused by real I/O infrastructure (DB query planner, index scans, network round-trips), not by in-process module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy the recommendation service against a real (or containerized) catalog store populated with 100,000 products and representative user data. Issue a recommendation request for a user with moderate history and measure wall-clock response time from request dispatch to full response receipt. Assert that the 95th-percentile latency across N repeated requests is below 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the module boundary between purchase-event ingestion and recommendation-state updates — the purchase signal must flow from the event handler through the user-model updater and be visible to the ranking function on the next call. A unit test of either component alone cannot catch a broken handoff. REAL-INFRA: An in-process event bus and in-memory user state store are sufficient; no real infrastructure needed to reproduce the propagation failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the ranking function would hide whether purchase signals are actually being applied. LLM-DEP: 'Deprioritize' reads as a rule (penalize purchased items) rather than a model re-training step. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected because the behavior is about state propagation, not ranking quality from an ML model; a deterministic penalty rule is the expected mechanism.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the purchase event handler, user-model updater, and ranking function together with in-memory state. Obtain an initial ranked recommendation list for a user. Emit a purchase event for the top-ranked product. Request recommendations again. Assert that the purchased product's rank has decreased (or it is absent) compared to its position in the initial list.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Non-determinism can originate anywhere in the pipeline — unstable sort in ranking, random serendipity injection without a fixed seed, hash-map iteration order in collaborative filtering, or concurrent state reads. A unit test of the ranking function alone can miss non-determinism introduced at module boundaries (e.g., the serendipity injector or the user-state assembler). REAL-INFRA: No real infrastructure needed; in-process module wiring is sufficient to expose ordering instability across two calls. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking individual steps would prevent the test from catching non-determinism introduced by their interaction. LLM-DEP: If the pipeline used an LLM, determinism would be unachievable — but the behavior requires it, confirming the algorithm is deterministic. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected because real I/O adds no additional surface for non-determinism beyond what in-process module wiring already exercises.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full recommendation pipeline (filtering, ranking, serendipity injection, pagination) with a fixed in-memory user state and product catalog. Call the recommendation function twice in sequence with no state mutations between calls. Assert that both calls return the identical list of product IDs in the identical order.
